coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescence, photothermal and magnetic properties of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots based ternary nanocomposite
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Gökalp, Ceren
Çıplak, Zafer
Getiren, Bengü
Yıldız, Nuray
coredata.dc:description: In present work, magnetic iron oxide@polypyrrole-nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs) ternary nanocomposite having high quantum yield and excellent photothermal activity was synthesized. The morphological and chemical structure of the prepared nanocomposites were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), magnetic hysteresis measurement (VSM), and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). To determine fluorescence properties, the nanocomposite was excitated at different wavelengths and the quantum yield of the ternary nanocomposite was obtained as 6.5 %. The effects of laser power density and concentration of aqueous dispersion of photothermal agents on the photothermal properties in the near-infrared (NIR) region (808 nm) were investigated. Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs ternary nanocomposite exhibited very high photothermal performance with excellent photothermal stability. The maximum temperature difference (ΔTmax) of aqueous dispersion (0.1 mg/mL) reached up to 19.1, 30.1, and 36.9 °C under irradiation of the 808 nm NIR laser at 1.5, 2.0 and 2.5 W/cm2 power density for 10 min. In summary, due to having excellent photothermal performance and high quantum yield, Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite is highly promising in simultaneous bioimaging and photothermal treatment practices.

head.ce:title: Photoluminescence, photothermal and magnetic properties of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots based ternary nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Ankara University, 06100, Tandoğan, Ankara, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Ankara University, 06100, Tandoğan, Ankara, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In present work, magnetic iron oxide@polypyrrole-nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs) ternary nanocomposite having high quantum yield and excellent photothermal activity was synthesized. The morphological and chemical structure of the prepared nanocomposites were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), magnetic hysteresis measurement (VSM), and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). To determine fluorescence properties, the nanocomposite was excitated at different wavelengths and the quantum yield of the ternary nanocomposite was obtained as 6.5 %. The effects of laser power density and concentration of aqueous dispersion of photothermal agents on the photothermal properties in the near-infrared (NIR) region (808 nm) were investigated. Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs ternary nanocomposite exhibited very high photothermal performance with excellent photothermal stability. The maximum temperature difference (ΔTmax) of aqueous dispersion (0.1 mg/mL) reached up to 19.1, 30.1, and 36.9 °C under irradiation of the 808 nm NIR laser at 1.5, 2.0 and 2.5 W/cm2 power density for 10 min. In summary, due to having excellent photothermal performance and high quantum yield, Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite is highly promising in simultaneous bioimaging and photothermal treatment practices.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the last decade, photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) have attracted a great deal of attention about cancer treatment and have become a promising alternative compared to conventional cancer therapy methods due to their limited side effects, minimal invasiveness, and low damage to healthy tissues [1–5]. Nanomaterials that efficiently transform energy from an external power source (magnetic field, laser, ultrasound, etc.) into heat with high efficiency have vital importance for PTT applications. For effective PTT treatment of tumors, PTT agents with high photothermal conversion efficiency, are still needed to be highly stable, delivered effectively, and accumulated over the tumor sites [6]. Conjugated polymers, such as polypyrrole (PPy) have high absorbance in the near-infrared region (NIR; λ = 700−1200 nm) and can effectively transform NIR radiation into heat energy. In addition, a having high surface area to volume ratio, PPy nanoparticles (NPs) exhibit high photothermal conversion efficiency, photothermal stability, and non-toxicity for PTT applications [7–9]. In addition to excellent PTT properties, conjugated polymer based carbon nanostructure nanocomposites have high conductivity because of the interphase regions. It is reported that the effective conductivity depends on the size, concentration, conductivity of the nanoparticles, and the properties of the contact regions between the nanoparticles [10,11]. Conjugated polymer based carbon nanostructre nanocomposites having high conductivity and mechanical stability are also promising materials for microelectronics industry and energy storage applications, such as batteries [12], supercapacitors [13] etc., photovoltaic devices [14], light emitting diodes [15], and biosensors [16].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are photoluminescent 0D graphene-based materials that are promising towards biomedical applications because of their biocompatibility, tunable, and stable fluorescence properties, low cytotoxicity, and excellent water solubility [17–19]. Due to their quantum confinement, luminescent GQDs which have excellent optical properties, high photostability, and solubility have extraordinary luminescence properties. Moreover, their fluorescence characteristics can be altered due to their adjustable band gap [20–22].

Therefore, GQDs which have become prominent in luminescence carbon materials have advantages compared to common semiconductor quantum dots such as CdHgTe, CdTe@CdSe, PbS, that are hazardous and toxic for both human health and environment [23]. GQDs can be prepared from a wide range of precursors with facile and cost-effective synthesis methods, and exhibit biocompatibility and low toxicity. Besides, GQDs show high stability compared to other luminescent materials. Due to all these features, GQDs have drawn a great deal of attention towards various fields, such as medicine, chemistry, and electronics [20,23–25].

Heteroatom doping causes the formation of small band intervals in GQDs and adjusting fluorescence properties due to the doping effect. Heteroatoms such as boron (B), nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), and fluorine (F) can be used in doping processes, but N-doping of GQDs has attracted a great deal of attention because of their excellent photoluminescence properties compared to GQDs [26,27]. N-GQD nanostructures obtained as a result of displacement of carbon with nitrogen in the GQD structure have more active regions since their chemical and electronic properties change, so they can be used in biomarking, photocatalysis, sensors, electrocatalysis, energy conversion, and storage materials [26,28–30].

Superparamagnetic nanostructures are efficient agents for targeted delivery of therapy agents to the tumor site. Among various superparamagnetic nanomaterials, iron oxide (magnetite, Fe3O4) is biocompatible, inexpensive, and suitable for clinical use. Furthermore, coating of PPy on an ultra-fine Fe3O4 surface leads to the formation of Fe3O4@PPy NPs that have both superparamagnetic and PTT properties that enable effective transportation of PTT agent to tumor sites [31,32].

In this study, it is aimed to prepare a PTT agent with excellent photothermal properties and stability, high magnetic and photoluminescence properties that enable delivery of the PTT agent to tumor sites, bioimaging and high PTT efficiency for tumor inhibition and ablation. Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs ternary nanocomposite was prepared with a facile self-assembly method for the first time and fluorescence, magnetic, and photothermal properties of the synthesized nanocomposites were investigated. The ternary nanocomposite exhibited combined PTT activity, fluorescence, and magnetic properties with very high stability that proved the prepared nanocomposites have a very high potential for PTT applications.

Citric acid (CA), pyrrole, urea, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), sodium dodecylbenzenesulfonate (SDBS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). 3.5 kDa, Spectra/ Por (Replingen/USA) dialysis membranes were used for the dialysis of NGQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: NGQDs were prepared with a facile bottom-up approach [33]. CA and urea were used as carbon and nitrogen sources in the synthesis of NGQDs, respectively. 1.148 g CA and 2.7 g urea were added to 24 mL deionized (DI) water and mixed until a clear solution was obtained. Then this clear solution was transferred into a 100 mL PTFE lined stainless steel autoclave and heated up to 160 °C for 12 h. At the end of the reaction, the mixture was cooled down to room temperature and filtered. To remove unreacted reagents, a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 3.5 kDa was used and the solution containing the NGQDs dispersion was obtained.

Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized with a one-step co-precipitation method using FeCl3.6H2O and FeCl2.4H2O as precursors. In the first step, the aqueous solution of FeCl2.4H2O and FeCl3.6H2O salts (1:2 M ratio) was prepared with ultrasonication for 45 min. At the end of this period, the mixture was placed in a mechanical stirrer and heated up to 80 °C under nitrogen atmosphere and then 10 mL of NH3 was added dropwise. After one hour of reaction time, the magnetic particles in the resulting solution were magnetically separated from the mixture, washed several times for purification, and finally dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C.

A 10 mL of aqueous dispersion of PVA (30 mg) was stirred for 4 h at 75 °C, then at room temperature for another 2 h. 10 mg SDBS was added to the cooled mixture and the resulting solution was sonicated for 45 min and then mechanically agitated. After 2 h, the solution containing methanol (2 mL) and pyrrole (40 μL) was added to the PVA/SDBS solution and the mixture was ultrasonically stirred for another 30 min. Finally, the aqueous solution containing 40 mg of FeCl3.6H2O (pyrrole: FeCl3.6H2O = 0.4 n/n) was added to the mixture and polymerization was sustained for 12 h [34]. The solution obtained by polymerization was centrifugated at 14,500 rpm. Later it was washed several times and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C. Fe3O4@PPy nanocomposite was also prepared as described above using Fe3O4 nanoparticles to comparison.

Ternary Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanocomposites were prepared with coating of core-shell Fe3O4@PPy surface with NGQDs using “self-assembly” method [35]. 12.5 mg of Fe3O4@PPy was dispersed in an aqueous solution containing different amounts (1−0.001, 1−0.02, 1−0.1 w/w) of NGQDs. The obtained solution was ultrasonicated in an ice bath for 2 h and filtered using a 47 mm diameter, 0.20 μm pore diameter filter membrane. After filtration, the solution was ultrasonicated for an hour and its structure was examined by UV–vis spectroscopy. The same procedure was repeated until the characteristic peak of NGQDs disappeared. The PPy-NGQDs binary nanocomposite was produced similarly via using PPy instead of Fe3O4@PPy.

Photoluminescence (PL) properties of the samples were investigated via stimulating nanocomposites with different excitation wavelengths (280−420 nm; Agilent Carry Eclipse). Quantum yields of the nanostructures were calculated using Eq. (1).

In this equation, Φ is the quantum yield, η is the refractive index, A is the absorbance, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, the subscript st and x refer to the reference standard and sample, respectively. In this work, quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution was chosen as a standard. The refractive indices of both NGQDs and quinine sulfate solution are 1.33 [36,37].

In the present work, photothermal performances of the prepared PPy@NGQDs, Fe3O4@PPy, and Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanocomposites and comparatively NGQDs and Fe3O4 were investigated using a 808 nm NIR laser equipped with a power-meter (Newport, 843-R) and a thermal camera (FLIR, E50) by utilizing various laser power densities (1.5–2.5 W/cm2) on different concentrations of the aqueous dispersions of the PTT agent (0.05 – 0.2 mg/mL).

Another important feature of the samples investigated for the PTT application is the photothermal conversion efficiency (ŋ). The η value is calculated by the given Eq. (2).

UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu-UV 1601, Japan) was used to monitor the optical properties of the synthesized nanostructures. Chemical and crystal structures of the samples were evaluated with FTIR (Shimadzu 8400-S, Japan), XPS analysis (PHI 5000 VersaProbe) and XRD (Rigaku Ultima-IV). TEM and HRTEM images were taken by Fei Tecnai G2 Spirit Bio (TWIN) (USA) and Jem Jeol 2100 F 200 kV HRTEM, respectively. VSM (Cryogenic Limited PPMS) was carried out for the investigation of the magnetic properties of the samples.

Fig. 1 a shows UV–vis spectra of NGQDs and NGQDs-Fe3O4 (1−1) and NGQDs-Fe3O4 (1–8) binary nanocomposites. Two characteristic peaks at 234 and 334 nm are clearly visible in the UV spectrum of NGQDs. The peak at 234 nm and 334 nm are attributed to the presence of CC bond of π → π* transition and carboxyl groups and the transition of the CO bond of n→π* transition, respectively [39,40].

Fig. 1b shows the UV–vis spectra of pristine PPy, Fe3O4 NPs, and Fe3O4@PPy (Fe3O4:pyrrole, 1:4) nanocomposite samples. For pristine PPy NPs that are stabilized in PVA-SDBS medium, the band at about 460 nm is attributed to π → π transition, while broad band (>700 nm) in NIR area corresponded to the bipolar band of PPy. Fe3O4 NPs exhibit a broad absorption band (330−450 nm) related to scattering of light by magnetic nanoparticles [41]. Fe3O4@PPy nanocomposite has the characteristic bands of UV radiation of magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles and the π-π transition and bipolar bands of PPy NPs. This indicates that the Fe3O4 nanoparticles were effectively coated by the polymeric structure.

PPy-NGQDs binary nanocomposite shows both the characteristic bands of π-π* transition and bipolar bands of PPy NPs with the characteristic peaks of CC bond (π→π* transition) and CO bond (n→π*) of the NGQDs. The presence of these characteristic bands indicates successful preparation of PPy-NGQDs structure (Fig. 1b).

Fig. 1c represents the UV–vis spectra of the ternary Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs that are prepared with different amounts of NGQDs loading. The ternary nanocomposite exhibits all characteristic bands corresponded to the components of the nanocomposite. On the other hand, the increase of NGQDs amount that were used for self-assembly process leads to increase in the peak intensity of CO bond (n→π*) at 334 nm due to the coating of more NGQDs on Fe3O4@PPy NPs surfaces.

The FTIR spectra of NGQDs structures prepared by bottom-up synthesis approach are given in Fig. 2 a. In the spectra, peaks at 3416 cm−1, 1350 cm−1, 1171 cm−1, and 1573 cm−1 corresponded to the characteristic bands of the −OH functional group, C–H tensile vibration, CO tensile vibration, and CC peak, respectively [28,42–45]. In addition to these bands, peaks of NH stress vibration, CO vibration in COOH, and CN stress vibration were also observed at 3194 cm−1, 1658 cm−1, and 1411 cm−1 wavenumbers, respectively. The presence of these peaks shows effective doping of GQDs with nitrogen atoms [28,29,40,44–46].

Fig. 2b shows FTIR spectra of pristine PPy, Fe3O4 NPs, PPy-NGQDs and ternary Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite. For FTIR spectra of PPy NPs, the peaks at 1543 cm−1 at 1458 cm−1 are attributed to the CC vibration of the pyrrole ring, and CN vibration of the pyrrole ring. Moreover, 902 cm−1, 1034 cm−1, 1165 cm−1 and 1288 cm−1 bands are corresponded to the characteristic in-plane CC vibrations, C–H deformation, NC strain, in-plane = CH of the PPy structure, respectively. In Fig. 2b, the FTIR spectra of PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite also consists of the characteristic peaks of the CC and CN vibrations of the pyrrole ring at 1550 cm-1 and 1465 cm-1. The peaks corresponding to = C–H vibration, C–H deformation vibration and CC vibration are at 1280.78 cm-1, 1041.60 cm-1, 910.43 cm-1, respectively. In addition to the peaks of the polymeric structure, the peaks at 3416 cm-1, 1658 cm-1 due to −OH, CO and vibration stresses of NGQDs shifted to 3441 cm-1, 1651 cm-1 due to the interaction between PPy and NGQDs.

In the FTIR spectra of pure Fe3O4, it was determined that Fe3O4 nanoparticles had characteristic Fe-O bond peak (580 cm−1). Ternary Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite includes all characteristic peaks of PPy and NGQDs with the Fe-O peak of Fe3O4 NPs. FTIR analysis shows the integration of NGQDs on Fe3O4@PPy surface, in accordance with UV–vis analysis.

XPS analysis, which is an important characterization method to determine the chemical structure of NGQDs, was given in Fig. 3 . The survey spectra of the sample consist of peaks of C1s, N1s and O1s corresponding to the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen bonds (Fig. 3). In addition, the prepared NGQDs sample was determined to be atomically 14.7 % doped with N atoms. A two-step synthesis mechanism was suggested for the preparation of NGQDs with a bottom-up approach [32]. In the first step of NGQDs synthesis from citric acid, citric acid molecules form a sheet structure and turn into graphene skeleton as a result of the dehydrolysis process. In the second step, amides react with adjacent carboxyl groups to form pyrrolic N structures attached to the five-member C-ring. As the reaction time increases, NGQDs and more N atoms continue to grow and join to graphene structure. In addition, some of the pyrrolic N groups convert into graphitic N groups that are bonded with three adjacent C atoms. High resolution N1s spectra of NGQDs is consisted of pyrrolic-N (∼399.1 eV) and Graphitic-N (∼401.2 eV) bonds (Fig. 3b) in accordance with the synthesis mechanism of the structure [33], whereas the spectra of C1s was determined to consist of 284.7 eV, 286 eV and 288.2 eV peaks corresponding to the sp2 carbon (CC / CC), sp3 carbon (CO and CN) and carboxyl groups in the graphene structure, respectively (Fig. 3c). Thus, the structures prepared using citric acid as the starting material indicate that N is effectively dopedwithin the lattice of NGQDs.

Fig. 4 shows HR-TEM images of NGQDs. It was determined that the 0D NGQDs structures with particle size in the range of 2−5 nm and the average size of approximately 4 nm were successfully prepared. HRTEM images prove that NGQDs having small sizes and a narrow particle size distribution were synthesized with a facile bottom-up approach, which is crucial for photoluminescence properties.

Fig. 5 shows TEM images of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, Fe3O4@PPy and ternary Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite. After Fe3O4 nanoparticles having an average particle size of 10.05 ± 2.01 nm were successfully coated by PPy, it was determined the Fe3O4@PPy nanocomposites had a narrow size distribution (40–100 nm) and a small average particles size (62.12 ± 8.12 nm) (Fig. 5a-b). According to TEM images of Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs (1−0.1) nanocomposites (Fig. 5c-d), NGQDs with the average particle size of 4 nm (Fig. 4), were successfully coated the surface of the Fe3O4@PPy nanocomposite by self-assembly with sonication.

Compared to the literature, the study of the magnetic properties of Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs ternary nanocomposite was conducted for the first time and the structures were analyzed by VSM at room temperature. VSM results of the analyzed nanostructures are given in Fig. 6 . Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite have zero “0” coercivity in accordance with superparamagnetic materials [47]. Ternary Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite exhibited a saturation magnetization of 25.2 emu/g (Fig. 6). When compared to pure Fe3O4 nanoparticles (58.87 emu/ g), the decreased saturation magnetization was expected because of coating of Fe3O4 surface with PPy and NGQDs. On the other hand, superparamagnetic Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite has high magnetic properties towards targeted delivery of PTT agents.

Fig. 7 shows PL spectra of the NGQDs structures excited at different wavelengths (280−420 nm). The PL spectra of NGQDs exhibit strong blue emission of NGQDs (440 nm) that is independent of excitation wavelength because of narrow particle size and regular surface of NGQDs [26,48]. In the PL excitation spectrum (PLE), the peak at 220 nm is related to the π→π* transition, while the peak at 345 nm corresponds to the UV peak at 334 nm. The maximum absorbance was obtained at 440 nm excitation wavelength [40]. Furthermore, the quantum yield of the NGQDs sample with strong blue emission was determined as 61.9 %.

The PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite was also excited at 320−380 nm and its PL spectra were obtained. It is determined that the nanocomposite exhibits PL independent of excitation wavelength and this is due to the homogeneity of the dimensions and surface defects of the structures. Quantum yields were determined as 2 % and 6.3 % for PPy-NGQDs (1−0.0014) and PPy-NGQDs (1−0.03) nanocomposites, respectively. PPy-NGQDs showed brighter blue emission under UV light (366 nm) with the increase in the amount of NGQDs covering the surface.

In order to determine the photoluminescence properties of Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanocomposites prepared with different amounts of NGQDs, PL spectra were obtained at various excitation wavelengths between 320−380 nm (Fig. 8 ). It was determined that the PL is independent of excitation as well as NGQDs and PPy-NGQDs structures that prove coating of Fe3O4@PPy surface with NGQDs homogeneously in size and surface defects. For ternary nanocomposite samples the quantum yields of Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs (1−0.001), Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs (1−0.02), and Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs (1−0.1) nanocomposites are determined as 0.6 %, 4.9 %, and 6.5 %, respectively. Furthermore, the Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite samples exhibited brighter blue emissions under UV light (366 nm) with the increasing amount of NGQDs on the surface of the nanocomposite. According to all these results, it was determined that NGQDs were successfully coated on Fe3O4@PPy surface and the Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanocomposites had high photoluminescence properties as well as superparamagnetic properties that are essential for both targeted delivery and bioimaging applications.

PTT is a promising approach for cancer treatment and antibacterial applications. For an efficient PT treatment, PTT agents should exhibit high PTT activity with having high photothermal conversion efficiency and photothermal stability. Recently, nanostructures have attracted a great deal of attention because of their high PTT performances. In this work, photothermal activities of synthesized PPy-NGQDs, Fe3O4@PPy and Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanocomposites and comparatively NGQDs and Fe3O4 were investigated by utilizing various laser power densities (1.5–2.5 W/cm2) on various concentrations of the aqueous dispersions of the PTT agent (0.05 – 0.2 mg/mL).

The absorbance values of Fe3O4 and NGQDs (0.1 mg/mL) at 808 nm were determined as 0.207 and 0.037, respectively (Fig. 1). The ΔTmax value for DI water is 1.6 °C at a power density of 2.5 W/cm2 for 10 min of irradiation (Fig. 9 ). While the ΔTmax values for Fe3O4 nanoparticles and NGQDs (0.1 mg/mL) were obtained as 11.7 °C and 10.0 °C, respectively.

The photothermal activity of pristine PPy, NGQDs, Fe3O4@PPy (1–4), PPy-NGQDs and Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanocomposites were investigated and time-dependent temperature profiles of the samples with a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL under laser irradiation at 808 nm wavelength at power densities of 1.5 W/cm2 and 2.5 W/cm2 are given in Fig. 9. It was detected that aqueous solution including PPy nanospheres (0.1 mg/mL) reached up to 49.8 °C and 58.2 °C in 5 and 10 min, respectively, at a power density of 1.5 W/cm2. For irradiation at 2.5 W/cm2 power density, the temperature of PPy nanospheres reached up to 78.1 °C and 88.4 °C in 5 and 10 min, respectively. As expected, the obtained maximum temperature increased with the increase of applied power density and photothermal performance of PPy polymeric structure was found to be extremely high when compared to the similar studies [28,50,51]. For Fe3O4@PPy, PPy-NGQDs and Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanostructures at a concentration of 0.1 mg / mL the temperature reached to 50.1 °C, 55.8 °C, and 40.8 °C, respectively after 10 min of irradiation at 1.5 W/cm2 power density. The temperature values of the same solutions reached up to 74.0 °C, 86.1 °C, and 59 °C, respectively, after 10 min of irradiation at a higher power density of 2.5 W/cm2.

When the photothermal performance of PPy-NGQDs and Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite samples compared to pristine PPy structure, it was observed that the obtained temperature after 10 min of irradiation was lower. The reason for the decreased photothermal performance of NGQDs doped nanocomposites is the coating of the surfaces of PPy and Fe3O4@PPy with NGQDs which have lower photothermal activity. Previous studies show irradiation of cancer cells beyond 48 °C for 4−6 min resulted in denaturation of proteins and DNA leading to removal of tumor cells [52–54]. Despite Fe3O4@PPy- NGQDs nanocomposite exhibited lower photothermal performance compared to PPy nanospheres, the ternary nanocomposite still has high and enough photothermal activity especially for the removal of tumor cells. In addition, having high magnetic and photoluminescence properties with high photothermal activity, Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs ternary nanocomposite is a promising agent for targeted delivery, bioimaging, and photothermal applications.

Fig. 10 shows the effect of laser power density on the temperature difference of binary PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite. For PPy-NGQDs (0.1 mg/ mL) the temperature increased to 55.8 °C, 73.5 °C, and 86.1 °C after 10 min of irradiation by increasing the laser power density to 1.5 W/cm2, 2.0 W/cm2, and 2.5 W/cm2, respectively. Maximum temperature increase values (ΔTmax) were determined as 33.6 °C, 51.4 °C, and 63.7 °C at 1.5, 2.0, and 2.5 W/cm2 power densities, respectively, for the solutions (0.1 mg/mL). The binary PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite solutions have very high ΔTmax values at all three power density values, which shows that the nanocomposite has a high photothermal performance.

Fig. 10b-c show the effect of NGQDs loading on the photothermal performance of Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanocomposites (0.1 mg/mL) prepared with different amounts of NGQDs irradiated at 1.5 W/cm2 and 2.5 W/cm2. At power density of 1.5 W/cm2, the Tmax values of Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs (1−0.001), Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs (1−0.02) and Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs (1−0.1) are 47.3 °C, 42.3 °C, and 40.8 °C, respectively, after 10 min of irradiation. Tmax values of Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs (1−0.001), Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs (1−0.02) and Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs (1−0.1) solutions were determined to be 72.0 °C, 61.8 °C, and 59 °C, respectively, by increasing the laser power density to 2.5 W/cm2. As expected, the temperature values of nanocomposites increased with increasing power density. Although photothermal performances of Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs coated with lower ratios of N-GQDs exhibited better PTT performance, Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs (1−0.1) nanocomposite still has high photothermal performance as well. Considering the magnetic and fluorescence properties of the ternary nanocomposite, the photothermal studies were further carried out with Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs(1-0.1).

Fig. 11 displays the effect of concentration (0.05 – 0.2 mg/mL) of aqueous dispersions of Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite samples on photothermal performance. After 10 min of laser irradiation, the temperature of the aqueous solutions of Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite at concentrations of 0.05 mg/mL, 0.1 mg/mL and 0.2 mg/mL at a power density of 1.5 W/cm2 was increased to 33.8 °C, 40.8 °C and 48.1 °C, respectively (Fig. 11a). While the temperatures of these solutions at concentrations of 0.05 mg/mL, 0.1 mg/mL and 0.2 mg/mL reached to 46.6 °C, 59.0 °C, 74.8 °C as a result of 10 min of laser irradiation at a power density of 2.5 W/cm2, respectively. At the end of 10 min irradiation of the solutions, ΔTmax values were found to be 19.1 °C and 36.9 °C at 1.5 W/cm2 and 2.5 W/cm2 power density at 0.1 mg/mL concentration, respectively. Fig. 11c shows the UV–vis spectra of the aqueous solutions of Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite at different concentrations. It was determined that the absorbance at NIR wavelength increased with the increase in nanocomposite concentration. Furthermore, this increase in the absorbance with the solution concentration at 808 nm showed a linear relationship (R2 = 0.9992) demonstrating the prepared Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite samples have excellent stability in aqueous media which is crucial for photothermal applications (Fig. 11d). Thermal camera images of the aqueous dispersion Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanostructures at a concentration of 0.1 mg / mL irradiated at 808 nm wavelength at 2.5 W/cm2 power density are given in Fig. 11e.

Fig. 12 a shows the heating and cooling period of the aqueous solution of Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite (0.1 mg/mL) irradiated by NIR laser at a power density of 2.5 W/cm2. (-lnθ)-t plot was obtained from the cooling data of the sample (Fig. 12b). τ value was calculated from the slope of (-lnθ)-t as 534.68 s−1, and using the Eq. (3), the ŋ value of the nanocomposite in this working condition was determined to be 33.5 %. In addition to effective heating profiles at low concentration values, the Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs ternary nanocomposite exhibited high photothermal conversion efficiency.

Fe3O4 nanoparticles having the lowest PTT performance also have the lowest ŋ value (% 17.2). In Table 1 , photothermal performance data of the nanocomposite samples produced in this work and similar nanostructure in the literature are shown. Increased photothermal performance in all nanocomposite samples containing PPy and the individual performance of pristine PPy showed that PPy containing nanocomposites have high photothermal performance. Compared to the literature studies, it was found that the photothermal activity of the PPy polymeric structure was extremely high [33,54]. Considering other studies, it can be observed that the synthesized NGQDs are compatible with the literature [47–49].

Photothermal stability is another essential performance criterion for a photothermal agent. Fig. 13 indicates the heating-cooling cycles of Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite (0.1 mg/mL) at a power density of 2.5 W/cm2. Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs (1−0.1) nanocomposite maintains its photothermal performance after 6 consecutive heating-cooling cycles that prove the ternary nanostructure has both high photothermal stability, high photothermal performance, and conversion efficiency.

In summary, Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs ternary nanocomposite which was prepared with a facile and low-cost method for the first time in the literature. The Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs exhibited high superparamagnetic and fluorescence properties with high photothermal performance which are dependent to composition of the nanocomposite. The Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs (1−0.1) nanocomposite exhibited high ΔTmax (36.9 °C) values at low nanocomposite concentration (0.1 mg/mL) at 2.5 W/cm2 NIR laser power density with high photothermal conversion efficiency (ŋ = 33.5 %). In addition, the ternary nanocomposite has excellent aqueous dispersibility and high photothermal stability. The excellent photothermal properties, strong NIR absorption of PPy based nanostructures even at low aqueous concentrations and low toxicity of the nanocomposite enables utilization of Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs for photothermal applications, such as cancer therapy. Moreover, as a good candidate for bioimaging and targeted delivery applications, the superparamagnetic Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs (1−0.1) nanocomposite has high quantum yield (6.5 %) and high saturation magnetization (25.2 emu/g). The ternary Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite is a promising material for targeted delivery, bioimaging, and photothermal applications. In addition, because of its unique morphological, physical and chemical features, the versatile Fe3O4@PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite have potential as electrode material for various electrochemical applications.

Ceren Gökalp: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Zafer Çıplak: Methodology, Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Bengü Getiren: Visualization, Investigation. Nuray Yıldız: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.
